title: Mixed conditional: meaning shift
question: If I had taken that job in Tokyo, I ___ Japanese fluently now.
explanation: Past→present mixed conditional: past perfect in the if-clause, would + base verb for the present result. 'Would have spoken' would imply a past result, not a present one.

title: Mixed conditional: meaning shift
question: If she weren't so stubborn, she ___ his advice when he offered it.
explanation: Present→past mixed conditional: her permanent personality trait (present) affected a past event. 'Would take' would imply a present/future result instead.

title: Mixed conditional: subtle distinction
question: If they had invested wisely, they ___ struggling financially right now.
explanation: Past→present: their past investment failure causes present financial trouble. 'Wouldn't have been' points to a past result, not the current situation.

title: Mixed conditional: subtle distinction
question: If he weren't afraid of flying, he ___ to the conference in Berlin last month.
explanation: Present→past: a permanent present trait (fear of flying) prevented a past action. 'Would go' wrongly implies a present or habitual result.

title: Mixed conditional: tricky
question: If I hadn't stayed up all night, I ___ so exhausted right now.
explanation: Past→present: the past action has a present consequence. The progressive 'wouldn't be feeling' captures the ongoing present state. 'Wouldn't have felt' would point to a past feeling.

title: Mixed conditional: tricky
question: If you were more organized, you ___ your passport before the trip last week.
explanation: Present→past: a permanent present trait (disorganization) caused a specific past event. 'Wouldn't lose' implies a general present habit, not the specific past incident.

title: Mixed conditional: tricky
question: If we hadn't adopted that dog, our house ___ so much quieter.
explanation: Past→present: the past adoption makes the house noisy now. 'Would have been' implies the house was or wasn't quiet in the past, missing the present focus.

title: Mixed conditional: tricky
question: If I weren't allergic to cats, I ___ the kitten my friend offered me last spring.
explanation: Present→past: permanent present condition (allergy) prevented a past action (keeping the kitten). 'Would keep' wrongly implies a current or future action.

title: Mixed conditional: tricky
question: If she had finished her degree, she ___ eligible for the promotion they're offering now.
explanation: Past→present: not finishing her degree in the past affects her current eligibility. 'Would have been' places the result in the past.

title: Mixed conditional: tricky
question: If he weren't colorblind, he ___ that the wires were different colors during yesterday's repair.
explanation: Present→past: a permanent present condition prevented a specific past observation. 'Would notice' wrongly suggests a general present habit.

title: Causative: have vs get
question: The manager ___ the report rewritten before the deadline.
explanation: 'Had + object + past participle' is the causative structure: the manager arranged for someone to rewrite it. 'Was the report rewritten' changes to passive with wrong word order. 'Did' and 'let' don't fit 'the report rewritten.'

title: Causative: get + to-infinitive
question: I finally ___ him to agree to the new terms.
explanation: 'Get someone to do something' means persuading them. 'Had him agree' would need 'had him agree' (bare infinitive) which implies arrangement, not persuasion. 'Got him to agree' captures the effort of convincing.

title: Causative: negative form
question: He ___ his suit altered — he did it himself.
explanation: The negative causative 'didn't have + object + past participle' means he didn't arrange for someone else to do it. 'Wasn't altered' changes the subject to the suit. 'Didn't to have' is ungrammatical.

title: Causative: question form
question: Where did you ___ your suit made?
explanation: In questions with 'did,' the base form follows: 'did you have your suit made?' The causative structure is 'have + object + past participle.'

title: Causative: get vs have nuance
question: After weeks of arguing, we ___ the landlord to fix the leak.
explanation: 'Got someone to do something' implies effort or persuasion, fitting 'after weeks of arguing.' 'Had the landlord fix' would suggest a simple arrangement without the struggle.

title: Causative: past perfect
question: By the time the guests arrived, she ___ the whole house cleaned.
explanation: Past perfect causative: 'had had + object + past participle.' The double 'had' is correct — the first is the past perfect auxiliary, the second is the causative verb. 'Had have' stacks two base forms and is ungrammatical.

title: Causative: tricky structure
question: He ___ his assistant send the emails while he was in the meeting.
explanation: 'Had someone do something' uses a bare infinitive — no 'to.' 'Got his assistant to send' would also work but requires 'to.' 'Made to' and 'let to' are wrong because make and let take bare infinitives.

title: Causative: get + past participle
question: You should ___ that mole checked by a doctor.
explanation: 'Get + object + past participle' is an informal causative. 'Have that mole checked' also works, but 'get' fits the informal advisory tone. 'Have to' changes the meaning to obligation.

title: Causative: tricky meaning
question: I had my wallet ___ on the train.
explanation: 'Had + object + past participle' can describe an unwanted experience (something happened to you), not just arranged services. Here it means the wallet was stolen from the speaker.

title: Causative: complex
question: She's getting her kitchen ___ before the holiday.
explanation: 'Get + object + past participle' for arranging a service. The kitchen is being renovated by someone else. 'To renovate' would need a person as the object, not the kitchen.

title: Inversion: not only (perfect)
question: Not only ___ every deadline, but he had also trained two new colleagues.
explanation: After 'Not only' at the start, subject-auxiliary inversion is required with the perfect auxiliary: 'had he met.' 'He had met' lacks inversion; 'had met he' misplaces the subject; 'he did meet' uses the wrong auxiliary for a past perfect context, all ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: no sooner...than
question: No sooner ___ the envelope than she burst into tears.
explanation: 'No sooner + past perfect + inversion + than' is the pattern. 'She had opened' and 'she opened' lack the required subject-auxiliary inversion; 'had opened she' places the subject after the main verb, which is ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: little did
question: Little ___ that the company would go bankrupt the following year.
explanation: 'Little' with a negative meaning triggers inversion: 'did they know.' 'They knew' and 'they did know' lack the inversion; 'knew they' puts the subject after the main verb without an auxiliary, which is ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: only after
question: Only after reading the letter ___ the gravity of the situation.
explanation: 'Only after' at the start of a sentence triggers inversion in the main clause: auxiliary 'did' before the subject, bare infinitive after. 'He understood' and 'he did understand' lack the inversion; 'did understood he' uses the past form after 'did' and misplaces the subject, which is ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: only when
question: Only when the results were published ___ how serious the problem was.
explanation: 'Only when' triggers inversion in the main clause. 'Did we realize' is the correct inverted form. 'We realized' lacks the required inversion.

title: Inversion: rarely
question: Rarely ___ such a moving performance.
explanation: 'Rarely' at the start triggers inversion: 'have I seen.' Present perfect fits because it refers to experience up to now. 'Did I seen' is ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: not until
question: Not until she apologized ___ willing to continue the discussion.
explanation: 'Not until' triggers inversion: 'was he willing.' 'Did he be' is never grammatical in English. 'He was' lacks the required inversion.

title: Inversion: hardly...when
question: Hardly ___ asleep when a loud crash woke me up.
explanation: 'Hardly + past perfect + inversion + when' is the standard pattern, parallel to 'no sooner...than.' 'I had fallen' and 'I fell' lack the inversion; 'had fell I' uses the past-tense form instead of the past participle and misplaces the subject, which is ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: on no account
question: On no account ___ the red button, no matter what the screen says.
explanation: Negative adverbial phrases ('on no account', 'under no circumstances') at the start require subject-auxiliary inversion: 'must you press.' 'You must press' lacks the inversion; 'must press you' and 'you press must' scramble word order and are ungrammatical.

title: Inversion: so...that
question: So impressive ___ that everyone stood up and applauded.
explanation: 'So + adjective' at the start triggers inversion: 'was the speech.' This structure emphasizes the degree of the quality.

title: Phrasal verb: separable tricky
question: She decided to ___ the meeting ___ until next week.
explanation: 'Put off' means to postpone. It's separable, so the object can go between the verb and particle. 'Put on' means to wear or stage; 'put up' means to accommodate; 'put out' means to extinguish.

title: Phrasal verb: inseparable
question: Who's going to ___ the children while we're away?
explanation: 'Look after' means to take care of and is inseparable — you cannot say 'look the children after.' 'Look into' means investigate; 'look over' means review.

title: Phrasal verb: 3-word
question: I've been trying to ___ a solution all week.
explanation: 'Come up with' means to think of or produce (an idea/solution). 'Come up to' means to approach or meet a standard; 'come along with' means to accompany; 'come up for' is not a standard phrasal verb in this sense.

title: Phrasal verb: particle shift
question: She needs to ___ her spending if she wants to save money.
explanation: 'Cut back on' means to reduce. 'Cut off' means to disconnect; 'cut out' means to stop entirely or remove; 'cut up' means to chop into pieces.

title: Phrasal verb: meaning shift
question: The detective is trying to ___ what really happened.
explanation: 'Figure out' means to understand or solve. 'Figure on' means to plan on or expect. 'Figure in' and 'figure up' are not standard phrasal verbs.

title: Phrasal verb: 3-word tricky
question: How do you ___ people who constantly interrupt you?
explanation: 'Put up with' means to tolerate. 'Put up to' means to encourage someone to do something (usually bad). 'Put down with' and 'put out with' are not real phrasal verbs.

title: Phrasal verb: separable mandatory
question: The company had to ___ fifty workers ___ due to budget cuts.
explanation: 'Lay off' means to terminate employment. It's separable, and with a noun object it can go between or after. 'Lay down' means to put down or establish rules; 'lay out' means to arrange or display.

title: Phrasal verb: particle changes meaning
question: We need to ___ the car before the long drive — the tank is almost empty.
explanation: 'Fill up' means to make completely full, which fits a fuel tank. 'Fill in' and 'fill out' mean to complete a form or document. 'Fill off' doesn't exist as a phrasal verb.

title: Phrasal verb: 3-word inseparable
question: I'm really ___ the concert next week.
explanation: 'Look forward to' means to anticipate with pleasure. 'Look up to' means to admire someone. 'Looking over to' and 'looking out to' are not standard phrasal verbs meaning to anticipate.

title: Phrasal verb: tricky separation
question: The teacher asked the students to ___ their essays ___ by Friday.
explanation: 'Hand in' means to submit. It's separable: 'hand their essays in.' 'Hand on' means to pass to the next person; 'hand off' means to transfer responsibility.

title: Cleft: it-cleft
question: ___ the broken lock that allowed the burglars to get in.
explanation: It-cleft: 'It was + noun phrase + that-clause' puts focus on the broken lock as the cause. 'What was' would start a wh-cleft and need restructuring. 'It were' has subject-verb agreement failure. 'Is been' is not a valid verb form.

title: Cleft: what-cleft
question: ___ really annoys me is people talking during movies.
explanation: What-cleft: 'What + clause + is/was + focused element.' 'The thing what' is ungrammatical — it should be 'The thing that.' 'It' would need a different structure.

title: Cleft: what-cleft vs it-cleft
question: ___ I need is a good night's sleep.
explanation: What-cleft: 'What I need is...' focuses on the complement. 'All what' is ungrammatical — it should be 'All that' or just 'All.' 'It' would need 'It is... that I need.'

title: Cleft: all-cleft
question: ___ she did was apologize and leave.
explanation: 'All + clause' is a type of cleft: 'All she did was...' emphasizes the limited nature of the action. 'What all' is redundant and non-standard in this position.

title: Cleft: the thing
question: ___ bothers me most is his complete lack of honesty.
explanation: 'The thing that' introduces a cleft focusing on the subject. 'The thing what' is non-standard grammar. 'What thing' would be a question form.

title: Cleft: emphasis on time
question: ___ last Tuesday that the committee finally reached a decision, not on Monday.
explanation: It-cleft focusing on a time expression: 'It was last Tuesday that...' The it-cleft emphasizes the corrected element. 'What was' and 'When was' would start questions, not cleft statements; 'There was' doesn't fit the cleft pattern.

title: Cleft: what-cleft with do
question: ___ she does every morning is go for a run by the river.
explanation: What-cleft with 'do': 'What she does is...' focuses on the activity. 'All what' is ungrammatical; 'The which' is not a valid cleft construction.

title: Cleft: it-cleft with reason
question: ___ because of the traffic that we arrived late, not because we overslept.
explanation: It-cleft focusing on a reason: 'It was because of... that...' This structure lets you correct a misconception by emphasizing the real cause. 'What was' cannot head this cleft pattern. 'It were' has subject-verb agreement failure, and 'Is was' stacks two finite verbs, both ungrammatical.

title: Cleft: complex what-cleft
question: ___ we need to do is reconsider the entire approach.
explanation: What-cleft with 'need to do': 'What we need to do is...' focuses on the necessary action. 'All what' is ungrammatical — use 'All that' or 'All' instead.

title: Cleft: negative it-cleft
question: ___ his talent that got him the job — it was his connections.
explanation: Negative it-cleft: 'It wasn't X that... — it was Y' is used to correct assumptions. 'What wasn't' cannot head this cleft pattern. 'It weren't' has subject-verb agreement failure, and 'Wasn't been' is not a valid verb form.
